preFix.title: Fitting a loft ladder
preFix.subtitle: Install a folding or concertina timber loft ladder in an existing hatch
preFix.excerpt: How to fit a folding or concertina loft ladder: checking the hatch size, fitting the frame to the hatch opening, installing the ladder mechanism, and adjusting the feet for the floor height. Covers both timber and aluminium slide-out types.
preFix.sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Building Regulations Approved Document K (stairs, ladders and ramps).

content.text: A loft ladder replaces a hinged hatch that requires a step-stool to reach. Most ladder kits consist of a frame that fits into the ceiling opening and a folding or concertina ladder that folds up into the frame when not in use. A standard domestic kit suits a floor-to-ceiling height of 2,500 to 3,000 mm; variable-leg versions extend to 3,200 mm. The ceiling opening must be at least 550 x 1,200 mm for a standard kit.

Measure the existing hatch opening and confirm the kit frame will fit. Most kits have an adjustable frame that fits openings between 550 x 1,100 mm and 700 x 1,350 mm. The frame must be supported by the ceiling joists, not by the plasterboard alone: check that the existing hatch opening has a trimmer joist at each short end and that the long sides either fall on a joist or are supported by a header across several joists.

Remove the existing hatch board. If the opening is smaller than the kit frame requires, cut the opening to the correct size with a jigsaw, ensuring any cuts between joists are supported by new noggings. Fit a trimmer joist at each short end of the opening if not already present.

per the kit instructions. Lift the frame into the opening from above. The frame sits on the ceiling surface; its upper edge rests on the top face of the ceiling material. Screw the frame to the trimmer joists and header through the pre-drilled holes. Check the frame is level in both directions with the spirit level before final tightening.
Step 3, attach and adjust the ladder

Attach the ladder to the frame according to the kit instructions. Unfold or extend the ladder to the floor. Adjust the bottom feet to the correct angle for the floor: most kits include adjustable rubber feet that can be set to match the pitch of the extended ladder. The feet should sit flat on the floor with the ladder at a comfortable climbing angle (approximately 65 to 70 degrees from horizontal).

Test the hatch opens and closes smoothly and that the ladder deploys and folds without binding. Fit the supplied insulation cover if included (most loft ladder kits include a draught-seal and insulation panel that closes against the top of the frame when the hatch is closed). If not supplied, fit a proprietary loft hatch insulation cover on top of the frame to prevent cold air dropping through the hatch into the hall below.

attrs.fix: Pack with thin timber shims behind the frame to close any gap, then fill and paint.
attrs.cause: Ceiling is not flat, or the frame was not screwed evenly.
attrs.symptom: Hatch frame not sitting flush to the ceiling

attrs.fix: Adjust the telescopic legs or feet to the correct length. If the height exceeds the kit's maximum, the kit is not suitable for the application.
attrs.cause: The floor-to-ceiling height is outside the kit's range, or the feet are not adjusted correctly.
attrs.symptom: Ladder feet not reaching the floor flat
